intro.title: Do you know bullying when you see it?
intro.subtitle: 7 short questions. Most people get at least 3 wrong — including the ones that matter most.

questions.prompt: Which of these is NOT bullying?

options.text: A classmate calls another classmate 'mota' every single day for months.
Two friends in the same group tease each other and both laugh.
A senior repeatedly takes a junior's lunch money.
A group of students systematically ignore one girl for an entire term.

questions.why: Mutual, consensual teasing between equals is not bullying. Bullying needs three ingredients together: repetition, intent to harm, and a power imbalance. Most parents — and many teachers — cannot name those three. That gap is where most bullying hides.
questions.world: Norway, Sweden, Japan, the UK, the US — all define bullying in statute so schools cannot quietly disagree.
questions.india: There is no national legal definition of school bullying in India. Each school decides for itself.
questions.stat: 1 in 3 Indian school children report being bullied. — UNESCO, 2019

questions.prompt: A boy is called a slur by the same group every PE class. He laughs along because he's scared. This is:

options.text: Just teasing — he's laughing.
Banter. Boys will be boys.
Bullying.
Only bullying if he complains.

questions.why: A victim laughing is one of the most common survival responses. The test is not the victim's reaction — it is whether the target has the power to make it stop. 'Boys will be boys' is one of the most damaging phrases in Indian parenting.
questions.world: South Korea's School Violence Prevention Act puts the duty on the school to investigate, regardless of whether the victim filed a complaint.
questions.india: With no binding law, most Indian schools act only when a parent escalates — and even then, optionally.
questions.stat: Over 70% of bullying incidents in Indian schools are never reported.

questions.prompt: How many school students in India report being bullied at least once a month?

options.text: About 1 in 20
About 1 in 10
About 1 in 4
About 1 in 3

questions.why: Closer to 1 in 3 — and that is only what gets reported. The real number is almost certainly higher.
questions.world: Countries that legislated saw drops in reported school-violence severity within a decade (e.g., South Korea post-2012 reforms).
questions.india: We measure bullying mainly through one-off academic surveys — not a mandated, recurring national audit.

questions.prompt: Which of these countries has NO standalone national anti-bullying law?

options.text: Norway
Sweden
Japan
India

questions.why: Most people assume India must have one. We have UGC anti-ragging regulations for colleges (2009) and POCSO for sexual offences — but school bullying itself has no binding national statute. We have CBSE circulars and NCPCR guidelines. They are advisory.
questions.world: Norway: Education Act §9A — every child has a legal right to a safe school. Sweden: schools must publish an annual anti-bullying plan, by law. Japan: 2013 Act after a student's suicide. South Korea: permanent record on the bully's file. UK: statutory duty on head teachers. US: all 50 states.
questions.india: Guidelines, not law. A school can follow them — or not.
questions.stat: India is among the most populous countries without a dedicated school anti-bullying statute.

questions.prompt: Your 12-year-old has been quiet for three weeks. You learn a group at school keeps mocking her accent. She begs you not to talk to the school. The best first step is:

options.text: Respect her wishes — kids must learn resilience.
Go straight to the principal tomorrow.
Listen, validate, document incidents with dates, and together decide when to escalate — but do not stay silent.
Tell her to hit back once so they stop.

questions.why: 'Let them handle it' is advice built for conflict between equals — not for bullying, where the power imbalance means a child cannot resolve it alone. Documentation matters: without a law, your written record is often the only leverage you'll have with a school.
questions.world: In Sweden and Norway, the school is legally required to act once notified. In India, you are negotiating goodwill.
questions.india: Parents are caught between 'don't be soft' and 'don't be a complainer.' Both extremes fail the child.
questions.stat: Children whose parents intervened with documentation report better outcomes than those whose parents went in cold or stayed out entirely.

questions.prompt: Children who are bullied for prolonged periods are more likely to experience: (pick all that apply)

options.text: Clinical depression and anxiety into adulthood
Significantly higher risk of suicidal ideation
Lower academic performance and school dropout
Chronic physical symptoms — sleep loss, headaches, weakened immunity
Difficulty forming trusting relationships, even decades later

questions.why: All of them. Bullying is not a 'phase.' It rewires a developing brain's threat-response system. The trauma outlives the bully, the school, and often the silence around it.
questions.world: Countries that legislated and tracked outcomes have seen measurable drops in school-violence-linked youth mental-health crises.
questions.india: We do not even systematically measure this at a national level.
questions.stat: Long-term harm is the rule, not the exception, when bullying is sustained and unaddressed.

questions.prompt: A 14-year-old's morphed photo is circulated on an Instagram group by classmates. Under CURRENT Indian law, the school is legally required to:

options.text: Investigate within 48 hours and report to police.
Suspend the students involved.
Provide mandatory counselling to the victim.
Nothing specific — there is no binding national rule. Action depends on the school's own policy.

questions.why: Sections of the IT Act and POCSO can sometimes be invoked AFTER harm is done — but there is no preventive, school-level legal duty.
questions.world: The UK's Education and Inspections Act 2006 places a statutory duty on head teachers to prevent bullying — including online — among pupils.
questions.india: Cyberbullying among students is one of the largest gaps in current Indian regulation.
questions.stat: Cyberbullying reports among Indian teens have risen sharply post-2020.

outro.tone: We do not shame wrong answers.
outro.fallbackForWrong: You're not alone — most people get this wrong. That's exactly why this needs to change.
